title: Feature flags are production code
hook: A flag is not a sticky note you slap on risky code. It is a branch in production. Name it, test it, own it, and delete it before it becomes archaeology.

blocks.html: The magic of a feature flag is simple: ship the code now, expose the behavior later. That gives you dark launches, internal testing, staged rollout, instant rollback, and fewer heroic deploy windows.

blocks.caption: That branch is live in production even when the new path is hidden.

beats.heading: Every flag doubles a path somewhere

blocks.html: A flag creates at least two realities: on and off. Sometimes it creates many more: user cohorts, regions, plans, experiments, internal overrides. If you do not test the important combinations, the rollback path can be the broken path.

items.label: minimum paths for every boolean flag
days before many release flags should be questioned

beats.heading: Put metadata next to the decision

blocks.html: A useful flag has a purpose, owner, creation date, expected removal date, and safe default. Without that, nobody knows if the off path is still real or just a museum exhibit with traffic.

blocks.title: zsh · stale flag audit
blocks.caption: This is the job that saves you six months later.

lines.t: next: assign owner or remove both paths

beats.heading: A flag is done when the branch is gone

blocks.html: Do not celebrate rollout at 100 percent and leave the switch behind. The actual finish line is removing the dead branch, deleting the config, and shrinking the test matrix back down. Flags are leverage. Unowned flags are debt with a UI.
